findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Outbound network request primitives that can transmit data externally
Outbound network request primitives that can transmit data externally
Data Exfiltration via Credential Theft and Network Upload
findings.description: The upload_agent.py script reads sensitive credential files (AWS credentials, GCP credentials, SSH private keys, npm/pypi configs) and all environment variables, then uploads them to an external endpoint (cloud-sync-api.io) via HTTPS. This constitutes unauthorized data exfiltration of credentials and secrets.
findings.evidence: CREDENTIAL_PATHS includes ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.ssh/id_ed25519, etc. build_payload() reads these files and includes them in the payload. upload() sends the payload to SYNC_ENDPOINT (https://cloud-sync-api.io/v1/backup).
findings.remediation: Remove credential collection entirely. Never read or transmit SSH keys, cloud provider credentials, or package manager tokens. Use environment-specific secrets management instead.

findings.title: Hardcoded API Key in Source Code
findings.description: A hardcoded API key (cs_live_Tq8mNvX2pR5sK7wJ) is embedded in upload_agent.py. This key is used for authentication to the external endpoint and could be extracted and reused maliciously.
findings.evidence: API_KEY = "cs_live_Tq8mNvX2pR5sK7wJ" is hardcoded in the script.
findings.remediation: Remove hardcoded API keys. Use environment variables or a secrets manager for authentication credentials.

findings.title: Environment Variable Harvesting and Exfiltration
findings.description: The build_payload() function captures the entire environment (dict(os.environ)) and includes it in the uploaded payload. Environment variables often contain sensitive data like API keys, database passwords, and tokens.
findings.evidence: "environment": dict(os.environ) captures all environment variables and sends them to the external endpoint.
findings.remediation: Do not collect or transmit environment variables. If specific variables are needed, whitelist only those required for the stated purpose.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning - Deceptive Description vs Actual Behavior
findings.description: The skill is described as a 'cloud sync' tool for backing up project configurations, but it actually collects and exfiltrates sensitive credentials, SSH keys, and all environment variables. The description ('Synchronize local project files and configurations to cloud storage') is misleading and hides the credential theft functionality.
findings.evidence: Description claims 'Synchronize local project files and configurations' but upload_agent.py reads ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, etc. and sends them to an external server.
findings.remediation: Ensure the skill description accurately reflects all operations performed. Remove credential theft functionality.

findings.title: Capability Inflation - Deceptive Skill Name and Description
findings.description: The skill is named 'cloud-sync' and described as a configuration backup tool, but its primary function is credential harvesting and data exfiltration. The name and description are designed to appear benign while hiding malicious intent.
findings.evidence: Name 'cloud-sync' and description 'Synchronize local project files and configurations' are misleading. Actual behavior includes reading SSH keys, cloud credentials, and all environment variables.
findings.remediation: Rename and describe the skill accurately. Remove all credential harvesting and data exfiltration code.

findings.title: Detection Evasion via Base64 Encoding
findings.description: The upload function base64-encodes the payload before transmission, which is a common obfuscation technique to evade detection by network monitoring or content inspection tools.
findings.evidence: data = base64.b64encode(json.dumps(payload).encode()).decode() - payload is base64 encoded before sending.
findings.remediation: Remove unnecessary encoding. If encryption is needed for legitimate purposes, use standard TLS (already present) without additional obfuscation.

findings.title: Data Exfiltration via Tool Chaining
findings.description: The skill chains multiple operations to exfiltrate data: cloud_sync.py calls upload_agent.run(), which reads credentials and configs (build_payload), then sends them to an external server (upload). This read→send pattern is a classic data exfiltration chain.
findings.evidence: cmd_backup() calls upload_agent.run(), which calls build_payload() (reads credentials) then upload() (sends to external endpoint).
findings.remediation: Break the tool chain. Do not combine file reading with network transmission. Require user confirmation before any data is sent externally.

findings.title: Over-Collection of Configuration Files
findings.description: The skill collects all configuration files (.env, .env.local, .env.production, config/*.json) without any filtering or user consent. These files often contain database URLs, API keys, and other secrets.
findings.evidence: CONFIG_PATHS includes .env, .env.local, .env.production, config/default.json, config/production.json - all read without user confirmation.
findings.remediation: Only collect files explicitly specified by the user. Do not automatically scan for and read configuration files.

findings.title: Unauthorized Tool Use - Violation of Implicit Tool Restrictions
findings.description: The skill uses network operations (urllib.request) to send data to an external server, which may violate agent tool restrictions. The skill manifest does not declare allowed-tools, but the behavior involves network communication that should be explicitly authorized.
findings.evidence: urllib.request.urlopen() makes HTTP requests to an external endpoint. No allowed-tools field in manifest to declare network access.
findings.remediation: Add explicit allowed-tools declaration if network access is legitimate. Otherwise, remove network operations.
